perturbation.original_text: 4. NIST shall only have a duty to protect the Proprietary Information which is disclosed to it in writing and is identified as “confidential ” by Discloser, or, if disclosed orally or in any other manner, if Discloser provides NIST with a written memorandum summarizing the Proprietary Information and designating such summary as “confidential ” within thirty (30) days of the disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. NIST shall only have a duty to protect the Proprietary Information which is disclosed to it in writing and is identified as “confidential ” by Discloser, or, if disclosed orally or in any other manner, if Discloser provides NIST with a written memorandum summarizing the Proprietary Information and designating such summary as “confidential” within sixty (60) days of the disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the timeframe for providing a written memorandum summarizing orally disclosed confidential information from 30 days to 60 days. This could be problematic if the proprietary information is related to a patent application, as patent law imposes strict deadlines for disclosing inventions and filing patent applications, some of which require filing an application within one year of the first disclosure, irrespective of confidentiality agreements. Increasing the time to provide a written record could increase the risk of not being able to prove the disclosure date. Patent Law is Federal.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 35 U.S. Code § 102 - Conditions for patentability; novelty
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification affects the Discloser's ability to establish an early disclosure date for patenting purposes. 35 U.S. Code § 102 dictates the conditions for patentability, requiring that the invention not be described in a printed publication, on sale, or otherwise available to the public before the effective filing date of the claimed invention. Delaying the written record could jeopardize the Discloser's patent rights if the invention is otherwise disclosed during the extended 60-day period.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 102 - Conditions for patentability; novelty | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 102 - Conditions for patentability; novelty U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Novelty; Prior Art.— A person shall be entitled to a patent unless— (1) the claimed invention was patented, described in a printed publication, or in public use, on sale, or otherwise available to the public before the effective filing date of the claimed invention; or (2) the claimed invention was described in a patent issued under section 151, or in an application for patent published or deemed published under section 122(b), in which the patent or application, as the case may be, names another inventor and was effectively filed before the effective filing date of the claimed invention. (b) Exceptions.— (1) Disclosures made 1 year or less before the effective filing date of the claimed invention.— A disclosure made 1 year or less before the effective filing date of a claimed invention shall not be prior art to the claimed invention under subsection (a)(1) if— (A) the disclosure was made by the inventor or joint inventor or by another who obtained the subject matter disclosed directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor ; or (B) the subject matter disclosed had, before such disclosure, been publicly disclosed by the inventor or a joint inventor or another who obtained the subject matter disclosed directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor . (2) Disclosures appearing in applications and patents.— A disclosure shall not be prior art to a claimed invention under subsection (a)(2) if— (A) the subject matter disclosed was obtained directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor ; (B) the subject matter disclosed had, before such subject matter was effectively filed under subsection (a)(2), been publicly disclosed by the inventor or a joint inventor
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text extends the timeframe for documenting oral disclosures of proprietary information from 30 to 60 days. This creates a potential contradiction with 35 U.S. Code § 102, which addresses conditions for patentability, specifically novelty. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this delay could jeopardize patent rights if the invention is publicly disclosed during the extended period. Both Scraped Law Snippets emphasize that an invention must not be 'patented, described in a printed publication, or in public use, on sale, or otherwise available to the public before the effective filing date of the claimed invention.' The increased timeframe in the modified text could make it harder to prove an earlier disclosure date, thus increasing the risk of losing patent rights if a disclosure occurs during the additional time. Therefore, the contradiction is impactful and falls under the definition of inconsistencies legal contradiction. The changed text creates a time-sensitive obligation that potentially violates federal law.

perturbation.original_text: 5. This Agre ement is effective as of the date of the last signature ( “Effective Date ”) and shall expire after one (1) year (“Expiration Date ”).
perturbation.changed_text: 5. This Agreement is effective as of the date of the last signature ( “Effective Date ”) and shall expire after five (5) years (“Expiration Date ”).
perturbation.explanation: The modification extends the NDA's duration from one year to five years. While not inherently illegal, a five-year NDA term, especially in certain highly regulated industries or concerning rapidly evolving technologies, may be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade under state laws or conflict with the Sherman Antitrust Act if it unduly restricts competition or employee mobility. This is true especially in states like California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. A five-year NDA, depending on the scope of the 'Proprietary Information' and the Discloser's business, could be argued as an unlawful restraint if it significantly impairs NIST's ability to conduct research or employ personnel who have similar knowledge.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the NDA duration from one year to five years, potentially conflicting with California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The Perturbation and Law Explanations suggest this extension could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially in California. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." A five-year NDA could significantly impair someone's ability to work in their field, thus creating a legal contradiction under California law.

perturbation.original_text: 6. NIST ’s duties under Paragraphs 3 and 4 of this Agreement shall expire three (3) years after the Proprietar y Information is received.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. NIST’s duties under Paragraphs 3 and 4 of this Agreement shall expire one (1) year after the Proprietary Information is received.
perturbation.explanation: This modification reduces the protection period of the proprietary information from three years to one year. If the proprietary information contains trade secrets that could provide a competitive advantage for more than one year, reducing the NDA protection period to only one year could be deemed insufficient under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA).
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings to protect trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the protection period for trade secrets to one year after receiving the information may be insufficient to provide reasonable protection, as defined under the DTSA. If the trade secret provides a competitive advantage that lasts longer, failing to protect it for a more appropriate duration could undermine efforts to prevent misappropriation and could be deemed commercially unreasonable, particularly if standard practice in the industry calls for longer protection periods.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the protection period for proprietary information from three years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation says that if the proprietary information contains trade secrets that could provide a competitive advantage for more than one year, reducing the NDA protection period to only one year could be deemed insufficient under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). The Law Explanation reinforces this, stating that reducing the protection period to one year 'may be insufficient to provide reasonable protection'. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly mention the length of time needed to protect trade secrets, it does discuss civil proceedings for violations of trade secret protections under 18 USC 1836. Therefore, reducing the protection period could be considered a contradiction of the DTSA, making the one year protection insufficient.

perturbation.original_text: 7. This Agreement may be terminated immediately by either party upon delivery of written notice of termination to the other party. Such termination shall not affect NIST ’s duties with respect to Proprietary Information disclosed prior to termination.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. This Agreement may be terminated by NIST only upon delivery of written notice of termination to the Discloser. Such termination shall affect NIST’s duties with respect to Proprietary Information disclosed prior to termination.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the termination clause to allow only NIST to terminate the agreement, and stipulating that termination *will* affect NIST's duties with respect to previously disclosed proprietary information, introduces an imbalance and creates a legal vulnerability. The Discloser is now unilaterally bound without reciprocal ability to end the agreement, and any protection of their proprietary information ceases upon NIST's termination, regardless of how recently it was disclosed. This could run afoul of basic contract law principles of fairness and mutual obligation and could be interpreted as an unconscionable term.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 208 deals with unconscionable contracts or clauses. This modification creates a situation where the terms are so one-sided as to be unconscionable at the time the contract is made. The Discloser lacks the ability to terminate the agreement while NIST retains that right, and the Discloser's confidential information loses protection immediately upon termination by NIST, rendering the agreement potentially unenforceable due to its unfairness.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: unconscionability | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you unconscionability Unconscionability is a defense against the enforcement of a contract or portion of a contract. If a contract is unfair or oppressive to one party in a way that suggests abuses during its formation, a court may find it unconscionable and refuse to enforce it. There are two types of unconscionability in contracts, procedural and substantive. Procedural unconscionability is present when during the contract’s formation, at least one party does not have fair/meaningful choice, there’s misrepresentation, or unequal bargaining power among other factors. Substantive unconscionability is when the contract’s terms unfairly benefit/harm one side such as extremely unequal price compared to value exchanged. A contract is most likely to be found unconscionable if both unfair bargaining and unfair substantive terms are shown. An absence of meaningful choice by the disadvantaged party is often used to prove unfair bargaining. For example, in J ones v Star Credit , the Court refused to enforce a contract that charged three times the market value of an appliance to a low-income plaintiff , with significantly unequal education and experience compared to the defendant , by finding the contract unconscionable. [Last reviewed in April of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities consumer protection business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The modified contract creates a legal contradiction by making the agreement one-sided and potentially unconscionable. The original text allows both parties to terminate, maintaining a balance. The changed text gives NIST the sole right to terminate while removing protection for the Discloser's proprietary information upon termination. This directly contradicts the principle of fairness and mutual obligation, as described in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that a contract may be found unconscionable and unenforceable if it's unfair or oppressive to one party. The change in the contract introduces a situation where the Discloser is significantly disadvantaged, which is a key indicator of unconscionability, since there is unequal bargaining power.
